did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an optimized deep learning model based on EfficientNet B7 enhanced with chaotic grey wolf optimization (CGWO) for multi-class skin cancer diagnosis and classification of dermatoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'RESEARCH ARTICLES' and presents original experimental work. It details the development of an optimized EfficientNet B7 model using chaotic grey wolf optimization (CGWO), describes the methodology including data pre-processing and model training, and reports new results such as a classification accuracy of 97.48% on a curated dataset of eight skin cancer classes.

title: Optimised EfficientNet for multi-class skin cancer diagnosis: integrating chaotic grey wolf algorithms
brief_summary: This study addresses multi-class skin cancer diagnosis by developing an optimized deep learning model based on EfficientNet B7 enhanced with chaotic grey wolf optimization (CGWO). The model was trained and evaluated on a curated dataset spanning eight distinct skin cancer classes, including actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, and melanoma. The primary evaluation approach involved assessing classification accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores, alongside a comparative analysis against state-of-the-art approaches. The proposed method achieved a notable classification accuracy of 97.48% on the balanced dataset. The results demonstrate that the optimized architecture effectively handles high-resolution dermatoscopic images and reduces computational overhead compared to existing methods.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin cancer diagnosis (eight distinct classes including actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, nevus, and others)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on multi-class skin cancer classification using an optimized EfficientNet B7 model. The output layer uses softmax activation to output a probability distribution over classes, selecting the class with the highest probability as the prediction. The study reports classification accuracy (97.48%), precision, recall, and F1 scores for eight distinct skin cancer classes.

datasets_used.name: Curated dermatological image dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset meticulously curated and validated by experts, consisting of dermatoscopic images. The paper does not specify a public repository name (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000) but describes it as a specific collection used for this study.

use.training: Used for training the optimized EfficientNet B7 model with chaotic grey wolf optimization (CGWO). The dataset was balanced.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (implied by the mention of validation accuracy/loss plots and balanced class distribution in training/test sets).
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance, achieving a classification accuracy of 97.48%.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy, precision, recall, F1 scores, and confusion matrices, with comparisons made against state-of-the-art approaches based on these metrics rather than statistical significance testing.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet, then fine-tuned for skin cancer classification

architecture_details.scaling_method: Compound scaling method using coefficient φ (phi) = 7
architecture_details.core_blocks: MBConv blocks (Inverted residual structures with depth-wise separable convolutions) with Squeeze-and-excitation modules
architecture_details.optimization_algorithm: Chaotic Grey Wolf Optimization (CGWO) used to optimize hyperparameters

training_details.hyperparameters: Optimized via CGWO; specific values not reported in text

layers.neurons: Equal to number of skin cancer categories (8)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Optimised EfficientNet B7 with Chaotic Grey Wolf Optimization (CGWO)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a uniform dimension of 600 × 600 pixels
Normalization of pixel values to a range of 0 to 1
Colour space conversion from RGB to HSV
Hair removal via greyscale conversion, morphological black hat transformation (3 × 3 kernel), thresholding, dilation, Gabor filters, and interpolation of identified hair regions using median values
Noise reduction using median filtering (smoothing filter)
Skin lesion segmentation using an ensemble of U-Net, U-Net++, and Attention U-Net architectures to generate consensus masks

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper states that the curated dataset has a 'balanced class distribution in the training and test sets' to enable fair evaluation, but does not specify the specific algorithmic method (e.g., oversampling, undersampling, or weighted loss) used to achieve this balance.

conclusions: The study developed an optimized deep learning model based on EfficientNet B7 enhanced with chaotic grey wolf optimization (CGWO) for multi-class skin cancer diagnosis.
The proposed model achieved a classification accuracy of 97.48% on a curated, balanced dataset spanning eight distinct skin cancer classes.
The integration of CGWO effectively optimized model hyperparameters, improving classification accuracy while reducing computational overhead and training time compared to existing methods.
The optimized architecture demonstrated superior capability in handling high-resolution dermatoscopic images and processing fine-grained variability in skin lesion features.
The model showed robustness in addressing challenges such as imbalanced datasets and inadequate augmentation through class balancing and effective pre-processing techniques.

The study contributes to advancements in early skin cancer detection technologies by providing a reliable and efficient tool for dermatologists and healthcare practitioners to assist in accurate and prompt diagnosis.
important_remarks: The dataset is described as 'curated and validated by experts' but is not a standard public benchmark (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000); specific repository details, total sample counts, and per-class breakdowns are not provided, limiting reproducibility and external validation.

The paper does not report specific numerical values for precision, recall, or F1 scores, only stating they were 'graphically depicted' in figures, which prevents quantitative assessment of class-wise performance.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; comparisons with state-of-the-art methods are based on reported accuracy metrics without statistical validation.

The specific hyperparameters optimized by the Chaotic Grey Wolf Optimization (CGWO) algorithm are not explicitly listed in the text, making it difficult to assess the exact contribution of the optimization strategy.

The study claims robustness against imbalanced datasets by stating the training and test sets have a 'balanced class distribution,' but does not detail the specific balancing technique used (e.g., oversampling, undersampling, or weighted loss).

The model was evaluated on a single, unspecified curated dataset; lack of external validation on independent, diverse datasets raises concerns about generalizability to real-world clinical settings with varying image qualities and populations.

While the paper mentions reducing computational overhead and training time, it does not provide quantitative metrics (e.g., FLOPs, inference time, or training hours) to substantiate these claims compared to the baseline models.
